sentences.text: Yeah.
So, in meantime, those guys [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went in for the-- begging for food.
And instead, to steal their-- [INAUDIBLE] I took off and went to the railroad.
The second time, when I come to this town, I met the-- this lady.
She told me already where the ghetto is.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And she told me, there's another side from the river.
It's not far from Vinnitsa, and I should go this and this direction.
So I went back already to the railroad to find my train to escape to Romania-- was there, Jewish people.
And so I am by the railroad, there, and there's a lot of Germany army were loading their-- their tanks, their cannons, their wagons, their trucks, and their cargo trains.
And from far away, I saw those guys, the farmermen-- they're looking for me.
So I was lucky.
A train moved out in the same direction, I jumped, and I went in the train.
And before nighttime, I reached the town, a little town, Brailov-- was before the border.
There, the train stopped, because there was a checkpoint, and the Germans came over to the train and asked for the documents for everybody.
I didn't have any documents.
But was people, the other people, like they released in Germany, there's that special documents to go to the other side of the river to Romania legally.
So they took us off of the train, and they're checking the documents.
They go first-- we'll go to older people, not to you young little boys, like me.
And I make-believe to have-- that I go to relieve myself to close to the cargo train.
I stay a minute, a minute-- I see they're reading the passports, about that, and I ran away in the field.
Ran out in the field.
They wouldn't run after me, but they were shooting, because they figured maybe the other people were also like me.
So they let me, one go.
So I'm walking in the field, and I met a-- a Ukrainian, or I don't know what nationality he is, whatever it was there.
And I ask him, 'How can I go through the border there to the other side?' He said, 'Oh, I know a lot of people like you.
But no, I'm going to work.
I come back from work, I'll take you.
You have to go special places-- otherwise, you drown, blah, blah, blah.' So I said 'OK,' and I knew already, the way he said it, that he's not an honest man.
So it was already night, dark.
He walk-- he walked away.
So he walked away.
I took a-- a half a mile, I run away-- was a little orchard with trees and everything, and I was hiding.
In meantime, I hear-- see lights, shooting.
And he probably went to them-- to the German gendarme to get me out.
And I was laying there until midnight.
Midnight, I came back to the station.
And-- on the way, also, a train pulled out.
I jumped on the train, until it comes on the border was a river, and there was a big bridge.
So I was afraid-- the train slowed down, so I was afraid to go and look again of documents.
I didn't know what cargo was even in the train.
But the reason why they slowed down is because it cannot go fast on a bridge on a river.
It slowed down.
So I was relieved when it was over the other side.
And the train pulled in, in a big town, Zhmerinka.
And the trouble was, the train was between military trains.
My train was coil in the train.
Yeah.
But it was stationed between the Germans-- on both side, Germans!
And they're patrolling.
Back and forth, they're patrolling, the Germans, patrolling.
So I have to wait until the-- like you see sometimes in the movies, they're going across?
Until they-- they spread themselves.
And I crawl under the train.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I crawl under the train, and, it was a garbage dump on the other side from-- from the railroad, and I jumped in the garbage dump.
And I was sitting until early-- early in the morning.
Early in the morning, ladies-- farmers, ladies, they bring their product to the market to sell.
I asked one lady where the ghetto is.
She told me, 'Come to me.
The market's near the ghetto-- I'll show you where.' Meantime, she gave me an apple to eat.
And I was cold-- winter, shivering.
This was in December already.
And, uh, I came in in the ghetto.
I figured they'll receive me, they'll accept me.
The opposite-- they wouldn't accept me.
Was in the Zhmerinka.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And this was the Romanian Jews and Ukrainian Jews, native, they were living there.
Was a ghetto.
So the leader, Goldman-- [INAUDIBLE] named Goodman-- Goldman-- Goldman or Goodman.
I don't remember it now.
Anyway, asked question-- he asked me if I have-- I have money, or gold, or something.
I told them I don't have-- even I had this.
I don't know.
I did-- I did have a golden watch from my mother and from my father-- American gold.
It's not really gold, but it is gold.
It's a different quality gold.
It was a cheaper gold.
And nobody told me what to do with this.
I-- I had this here under my knee, here and my knee here, under my arms.
I was, with the handkerchief, tied it up, and that was all.
I had nobody told me to do it.
I do it-- I did it.
And, uh, I'm telling you the story, but this is important later on.
So, uh, he said to me, go to the little village there.
There is also a little ghetto, maybe they will receive you, [NON-ENGLISH].
So, OK.
I went to the little town.
I went to the little town, there were nice people, but that only three families.
If the Romanian gendarmes-- Romania already there-- they'll find out, they'll take you away.
So I cannot keep you here.
So they didn't tell me there's another ghetto, another couple miles.
So I didn't know.
I went back.
I went back to this ghetto, so he beat me up.
Who beat you up?
The, head of the ghetto.
Yeah.
He said, 'If you don't leave it-- I give you one chance.
If you don't leave it, I turn you over.' I start to cry.
Was there, a Jewish woman from-- native, from the ghetto.
She wants to take me in.
He wouldn't let her.
She wants me to get me to stay.
I don't know.
Because he was with the gendarmes, with the Germans, with everything.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I begged him, so he released me, throw me out the ghetto.
And I start marching.
Then I met again those ladies, the farmer, and she told me that about 20 miles, there's another ghetto.
And there, it's an open ghetto, not enclosed.
So here was with wires and everything.
There, they will accept me.
So I walked to-- I walked out, I walked to the ghetto.
And no, no-- I went to the railroad.
I wanted to go back already, to Russia, and the other side.
So one of the commanders from the station, railroad, he was suspicious.
He was following me.
[INAUDIBLE] I was afraid.
I went out of town.
He follows me out of town.
But I go faster.
He goes faster.
I start to run, he runs with me, till-- till I was running, running.
He wants to catch me, probably to turn me over to the Germans.
And I fell, he fell over me.
But he give up, because I was younger than him.
He left me, was night.
And I went into a shank, a little shank I find, near a house.
I find some boots.
I was barefoot.
I put on boots and everything.
The following morning, I got up and went to different ghetto, Murafa.
On the way--
Yeah.
On the way, I met another guy what escaped the same place what I lived from Poland.
He about my age.
He's in Israel now.
That's a different story.
He's-- he had a mother.
He had a mother in Israel, and his father was divorced.
So he came later on through the Red Cross to Israel from Romania.
And he was sick in the hospital, and the mother was the nurse in the same hospital.
[LAUGHS]
That's a different-- [LAUGHS] different story, too.
So anyway, I came to the-- the other town, Murafa.
We had some problems on the way to go to rather than-- but it's not time for this to tell you.
There was a nice man there at the other ghetto, the Dr.
Baichal.
He's from Suceava some-- from Romania.
But expelled, also, from Romania.
And he had received us.
He gave us a place to stay.
He paid for our boarding.
And I remember, was a doctor, good man, took us in to give us the first meal.
We had soup.
But I didn't have soap for-- for two, three years.
Was like manna.
And we were-- I were in-- and this was under Romanian occupied place, where they call this Transnistria.
Transnistria-- I didn't know what Transnistria means until I came to United States.
I know-- I learned-- in the United States, I learned 'trans' means the other side.
There was a river, Dniester, after Romania. it was the border between Russia and Romania.
This part, where they gave it to the Romanians, they called it Transnistria.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] So call this part 'the other side of the Dniester, [INAUDIBLE].
It was labor camps there, people starving, dying, day and night.
And, I was in this ghetto until a certain time, and then the ghetto couldn't pay for me.
So I had-- in the place where I was staying, was a guy, was a pocket-picker.
So if I was babysitting for the children, and he used to give me a piece of bread.
And then once they grabbed people for work, for a lager, labor lager.
And it's a native in the same town-- he had money to buy himself out with gold.
So we have to fill in the 300 people.
So they came, and the place where we're staying is a bathhouse.
But I-- I hid myself in a kettle, and the people wouldn't give me out.
But the police wouldn't give out-- they knew I'm there.
They knew me, because I'm-- I'm a refugee.
And they're looking for me to take the place for the other guy.
They wouldn't give up.
We negotiated for six loaves bread, a jacket, and a pair of boots.
I took the other guy's place, and I sold myself to go away in the labor camp.
So I went to the labor camp.
I was there in the labor camp about two or three weeks.
Worked-- were digging turf and other items.
And a lady appeared, what I knew her from another ghetto.
She was a heroic lady.
She was a mass grave-- she came out from the mass grave with the whole town-- Odessa, near Odessa.
They execute the town, and she was buried under the people.
Nighttime, they didn't have chance to cover the people, so she went out.
And then she became a real heroic lady.
She was going save people from camps, take them out--
I don't-- I don't remember, again.
And anyway, so, she came into this camp, what I worked, in the town of Tul'chin, labor camp.
She came in, and she saw me.
So I ask her what she's doing there.
She said she can-- she got paid to pick somebody up from this camp to take out.
I said, 'Why don't you take me?' She said, 'Yeah.' So I sold the bread, what I had-- something to somebody.
I sold my jacket, and I got some money.
And I paid her.
And she told me, the way-- because we were working, and not in camp-- outside.
We're digging turf.
She said, nighttime, when you go back from work, this and this place, and this-- this forest, you jump out.
And you will hear a signal.
You'll jump out.
And so I did.
And we worked 70-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kilometer, all night.
And we come back to the other town, the ghetto, what I tell you, we came in.
But accepted name is Murafa.
Murafa.
And then-- then, anyway, I don't know what happened.
Through the Red Cross or something, we're supposed to register to go to orphanage people to go to Palestine.
So I register myself, and the same guy, this-- this fellow what followed me.
So I went, to Mogilev, the town what we're supposed to deport from there.
So I didn't know, the registers-- only, they accept people until 16 years old.
And I didn't know.
I-- I told him already, 'I'm 16 and a half years old.' They wouldn't accept me.
I went through all, you know, the check-up, and disinfection, and a Turkish bath.
Matter of fact, it's the first time for years I was in a Turkish bath, and I didn't want to go out.
And-- and the same time, the-- the men all left, and I was sitting there.
All of a sudden, they let in about 40 people, naked people!
I didn't think about girls.
But naked people, they put them in.
And they start yelling, screaming.
[LAUGHS]
So I got scared.
I got scared, and I come over to the door, because the policeman want to go by.
They wouldn't let me-- the policeman, because disinfection-- to clean, you know, under the arms and everything.
And they gave me clothes and everything.
And then we went to the main office to register.
They wouldn't accept me.
Because I was half a year later.
But this guy, him-- and he went through Romania, he went to Palestine.
Through the Red Cross.
I don't know who sponsored this.
[INAUDIBLE] I went back to the ghetto, then I worked on a farm for tobacco.
And then the-- we had already partisans coming in openly.
We knew the front is closer to-- this, I'm talking, already, it was 19-- beginning 1944.
And, it was terrible thing.
People dying from hunger and this.
And it's hard to describe.
And by March '44, the Russian army came in and liberate.
And I had the pleasure even, they gave me weapon to shoot on the Germans on the front across the street.
I showed them where they digged in, and everything.
And I took off, and the way back was my march back to my town to see if everybody survived.
So I came back to the-- to the Vinnitsa-- you know, the town where I was with the-- with the street gangs.
Where are you going first thing?
You're going to the police to find [INAUDIBLE].
I wanted to sleep someplace.
I come into police.
The leaders from the police was those street gangs.
Before they gave me out, they kissed me and hugged me.
They're friends.
I didn't want to make a deal from this.
The others, they eat.
They gave me food, eat, and drink.
And I slept over, and I said goodbye the following day.
I went the way home, back.
The way home back, I came to a town, [NON-ENGLISH].
There in [NON-ENGLISH], they took me off.
There was the Polish army.
They took me off, they-- they figured me, no documents or anything, that I'm a spy.
They took me to the KBG and to Berdychiv.
And-- and then they released me.
So I was travelling-- because I was looking the way home, then I was travelling with another group, with girls in the train.
And also, they said I'm already 18 years old, that I have to go to the Russian army.
And so I ask him, 'Give me a place to stay.' So he said, 'No, you come two weeks later.' Two weeks later?
So on the way back, I tore up my papers, I make me two years younger.
And I went to the town of Rovne.
Town of Rovne was the front.
Covered was the front.
My town wasn't yet liberated, so about three months, I was working in this town of Rovne.
And then I went to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my town.
When I came to my town, I find a couple survivors like-- he knows Nettle Sopol, and Moshe Blumen, Pinya Lipshitz.
And this-- the boys-- about three, four boys.
And then a-- a couple, about 10-- altogether 10, 12 people, like you mentioned in the book, survivors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from-- from there, I went to-- to Pole.
Poland, I met a writer, [?
Ellen Berg ?].
He wrote about the war, about [NON-ENGLISH] book, [NON-ENGLISH], all the stories.
And from then, I went-- one of my relatives, what I tell you survived.
They lived in Russia.
They came with a truck.
They took me to Russia, and I worked in Russia for two years as a truck driver.
And all of a sudden, I find out [BACKGROUND NOISE] that people were-- were Polish citizens-- the Russians let them out.
They could go to Germany, Poland.
So-- and-- but I was deep in Russia, working.
So I have to escape, but same way like escaped from the ghetto, I escaped back.
And I came to Lwow.
Then I was arrested there, because I didn't have proper documents.
I was sitting in prison.
And one day, there was a Jewish leader from the-- from the Jewish committee.
They allowed, at that time, the Russian allowed him.
He went to the KBG.
He bought-- bought me out from there-- gave them presents, something.
And I came over to the railroad station.
And there was a train ready to leave for Poland.
They took with documents, but I didn't have any documents.
So the man what was responsible for this train asked me my name.
He asked me what I want.
I said, I want to go to Poland.
I said, where I am?
He said, from Luboml.
You have any documents?
No.
What's your name?
I said, Getman.
You're Getman?
Your father had the shoe store?
Yes.
I know your whole family.
He happened to be from my town.
I didn't know him, but he know me.
He put me, write me down the list, I went into the train.
And went up to-- to Poland.
In Poland, I belong to Kibbutz Ichud.
I was organize up in another kibbutz.
In Poland.
And from Poland then, we went through Czechoslovakia to Austria.
And I lived in a DP camp in Steiermark and [NON-ENGLISH], and, uh, Admont.
And at Admont I find out where my brother-- that he's-- through the Red Cross, that he is a prisoner, but a British.
Uh, that left for Palestine.
Yaakov.
As I find out that he's, uh, sent away from Palestine to [INAUDIBLE].
And then I volunteered-- in 1948, I volunteered to go fighting for Palestine, for independent of Israel.
I joined their [NON-ENGLISH] I was questioned and everything, and they accept me.
And the [NON-ENGLISH] was still-- was the Altalena story, what that we took us down from the front and brought us to the city.
Then I joined the IDF, Israeli army, and I was for three years in the Israeli army.
I took part in the Sinai campaign, and the independence.
And in 1959, I left for United States.
One man, Mr.
Sopol, he went.
[LAUGHS] he had a grandmother in United States.
He left to see his grandmother.
And he wrote me letters that he is only six months in America, he's already has a Cadillac.
And he went to Mexico, and went to Canada.
So I figured if Mr.
Sopol can do it, I'll do it, too.
And meantime, I was struggling in Israel.
They couldn't have a job, it was the time was very bad.
You couldn't get married, because you are a greenhorn.
If you don't have a house, you cannot find a girl.
And girls, weren't too many in Israel either way, because after the war, most men survived than girls.
Yeah.
Girls survived-- for some reason, they couldn't identify them, this mean in the ghettos.
But to escape-- like to Russia, to escape and everything, was men escaping, not girls.
So in campaign, was a problem of this, too.
You need to find a girl and to settle down.
And if you want to settle down, you have to be supportive.
I didn't have no trade, not anything.
And I think it was--
So in meantime, I didn't want to go married to the United States.
I figured maybe I wouldn't like it.
I-- I don't want to be-- and be married.
So I came here to United States.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what-- I came in '59?
And in '62, I met my wife in a bus going to Orchard Beach.
[LAUGHS] You met her on the bus?
On a bus going to the Bronx.
I was introduced through a girlfriend there.
And in 1962, we married.
And '63, when my Francine daughter was born.
And '65, Arlene was born.
And '72, the two sons, Mark and Steven were born.
Twins.
So all the years, I was silent.
I wasn't talking.
I was busy to put my life back together.
But I'm getting old, and it was time to tell the story.
I couldn't tell you anything in those two hours.
But, uh, everyday could be a story.
And you have to be a really, a scholar to describe everything.
I'm just telling you word per word some important occasions what happened.
I'm coming out with a book.
I hope-- but this at the end of the year.
It's more details.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it's very important.
People denying, and like it's never happened.
And you see me-- maybe I'll show you pictures.
And the pictures, you see how many people where they're not alive.
So how come I have the pictures?
Because those pictures were sent to-- to two brothers, United States, with my father.
My father had two brothers, and my one brother would emigrate to Palestine before the war.
That's why I had the pictures.
A lot of my friend don't-- only visualize are their parents.
They don't have any more pictures.
Only visualize and everything.
My occupation is, I was-- I worked as a helper to a mechanic for restaurant equipment and supplies.
Then my boss went bankrupt, and I took it over from the bankruptcy in the auction for $4,000.
And so to this day, I'm a restaurant equipment supplier.
Now I have lots of experience and-- and business, and mechanical work.
And one of my sons is keeping up with those business.
Hobbies-- hockey.
Swimming.
[LAUGHS] Soccer.
Some art.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Shows.
I am involved in-- in my temple.
Right now this year, I am the president of the men's club at my temple.
I like to be active.
I like to help people.
And I did help.
And I don't want to brag by this, because I do this from my own will.
I feel good if I can help people.
I hope--
